summary: Spanish Cubist painter. Most commonly identified by his Portrait of Picasso and Still Life with Checkered Tablecloth. Third major Cubist after Picasso and Braque. Pioneer of Synthetic and Crystal Cubism. Died at 40 of renal failure. Also known for The Sunblind, Newspaper and Fruit Dish.

works.name: Portrait of Picasso (1912)
works.description: Cubist portrait showing Picasso holding a palette, dominated by diagonal lines. Painted at end of Analytic Cubism period. Modigliani also painted a portrait of Gris.

clues.clue: Subject wears high-collared jacket and holds palette; brown background with parallel diagonal lines
examples: painting Picasso holding a palette in a work dominated by diagonals
the subject of a portrait by this artist wears a high-collared jacket and holds a palette

works.name: Still Life with Checkered Tablecloth (1915)
works.description: Still life featuring a lemon and newspaper; objects form the shape of a bull. Punningly shows only 'E-A-U' on a bottle of Beaujolais (turning wine into water).

clues.clue: Objects form the shape of a bull (symbol of Spain); punningly shows only 'E-A-U' on a bottle of Beaujolais
examples: objects on this artist's Cubist Still Life with Checkered Tablecloth form the shape of a bull
punningly turns wine into water by showing only the letters "E-A-U" on a bottle of Beaujolais

works.name: Fantomas (1915)
works.description: Synthetic Cubist painting in which half of Le Journal newspaper blends in with the table on which it rests. Exemplifies Synthetic Cubism's emphasis on flat, colorful planes over decomposition of the subject.

clues.clue: Half of Le Journal newspaper blends in with the table it rests on; exemplifies Synthetic Cubism's flat colorful planes
examples: this artist's 1915 painting Fantomas shows half of Le Journal newspaper blending in with the table on which it rests
Fantomas by Juan Gris displays this period's emphasis on flat, colorful planes over decomposition of the subject

works.name: Other Works
works.description: The Painter's Window: guitar, palette, panel with pears, and playing cards obscure the title window. The Mountain Le Canigou: simplified to a brown triangle; guitar extends to a black-and-white upside-down hat or toilet bowl. Man in the Cafe: alludes to Picasso and Apollinaire with letters 'PIC' and 'AP.' Violin and Checkerboard: sheet music lying next to a checkerboard at the bottom. Cards and Glass of Beer (1913): two strips of orange wallpaper on left; blue silhouette traced around the glass. Still Life before an Open Window: Crystal Cubism period. Still Life on a Chair: referenced by Dali for The Hallucinogenic Toreador. Designed cover for L'Humoriste on which an angel rides an elephant. Joseph Cornell made a 'Homage to Juan Gris' series.

examples: This artist simplified the title landmark to a brown triangle and emphasized a guitar which extends to a black and white upside-down hat or toilet bowl in his painting The Mountain 'Le Canigou'

clues.clue: Still Life on a Chair was referenced by Dali as he composed The Hallucinogenic Toreador

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Spanish Cubist painter born José Victoriano González-Pérez. Third major Cubist alongside Picasso and Braque. Coined the terms 'analytic cubism' and 'synthetic cubism.' Pioneer of Crystal Cubism. Called painting 'a sort of flat, coloured architecture' in a Sorbonne lecture. Frequently incorporated Le Journal newspaper in still lifes. Designed sets for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes. Designed a cover for L'Humoriste on which an angel rides an elephant. Modigliani painted a portrait of him (head cocked to the right). Died at age 40 of renal failure in 1927. Joseph Cornell made a 'Homage to Juan Gris' series of boxes.

recursive_suggestions: Synthetic Cubism vs Analytic Cubism — frequently asked about
comprehensive_summary:
Juan Gris (1887–1927) was a Spanish Cubist painter, born José Victoriano González-Pérez, and the third major Cubist alongside Picasso and Braque. His Portrait of Picasso (1912) shows Picasso in a high-collared jacket holding a palette against a brown background dominated by parallel diagonal lines. It was painted at the end of the Analytic Cubism period — Gris himself coined both the terms 'analytic cubism' and 'synthetic cubism.' His Portrait of Picasso bears the inscription 'Hommage à Picasso.'

His Still Life with Checkered Tablecloth features objects that form the shape of a bull (symbol of Spain) and punningly turns wine into water by showing only 'E-A-U' on a bottle of Beaujolais.

Gris was a major exponent of Crystal Cubism and pioneered Synthetic Cubism around 1913. He called painting 'a sort of flat, coloured architecture' in a Sorbonne lecture (he also delivered the lecture 'On the Possibilities of Painting'). He frequently incorporated Le Journal newspaper in his still lifes, and often paired guitars with newspapers in works like Newspaper and Fruit Dish.

Other works: Fantomas (1915) — half of Le Journal newspaper blends with the table it rests on, a key Synthetic Cubist work; The Painter's Window — guitar, palette, pears, and playing cards obscure the title window; The Mountain Le Canigou — the landmark simplified to a brown triangle, with a guitar extending to an upside-down hat or toilet bowl; Man in the Cafe — contains letters 'PIC' and 'AP' to allude to Picasso and Apollinaire; Violin and Checkerboard — sheet music lying next to a checkerboard; Cards and Glass of Beer (1913, Columbus Museum of Art) — two strips of orange wallpaper on the left, blue silhouette traced around the glass; Still Life before an Open Window (Crystal Cubism period); Still Life on a Chair — referenced by Dalí as he composed The Hallucinogenic Toreador. He also designed a cover for L'Humoriste on which an angel rides an elephant.

Gris designed sets for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes. Modigliani painted a portrait of him (head cocked to the right). Joseph Cornell made a 'Homage to Juan Gris' series. Gris died at age 40 of renal failure in 1927.
